URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cisgenesis

SUMMARY:
Cisgenesis is a product designation for a category of genetically engineered plants. A variety of classification schemes have been proposed.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Cisgenesis

**Cisgenesis** is a product designation for a category of [genetically engineered](/wiki/Genetic_engineering "Genetic engineering") plants. A variety of [classification schemes](/wiki/Classification_scheme "Classification scheme") have been proposed[[1]](#cite_note-Nielsen03-1) that order [genetically modified organisms](/wiki/Genetically_modified_organism "Genetically modified organism") based on the nature of introduced genotypical changes, rather than the process of genetic engineering.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/21/Cisgenicpotatoes.JPG/250px-Cisgenicpotatoes.JPG)

Cisgenesis (etymology: cis = same side; and genesis = origin) is one term for organisms that have been engineered using a process in which genes are artificially transferred between organisms that could otherwise be conventionally bred.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Schubert06-3) [Genes](/wiki/Genes "Genes") are only transferred between closely related organisms.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Nucleic acid sequences must be isolated and introduced using the same technologies that are used to produce transgenic organisms, making cisgenesis similar in nature to transgenesis. The term was first introduced in 2000 by Henk J. Schouten and Henk Jochemsen,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and in 2004 a PhD thesis by Jan Schaart of [Wageningen University](/wiki/Wageningen_University "Wageningen University") in 2004, discussing making [strawberries](/wiki/Strawberry "Strawberry") less susceptible to *[Botrytis cinerea](/wiki/Botrytis_cinerea "Botrytis cinerea")*.

In [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), currently, this process is governed by the same laws as transgenesis. While researchers at Wageningen University in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") feel that this should be changed and regulated in the same way as conventionally [bred plants](/wiki/Plant_breeding#Classical_plant_breeding "Plant breeding"), other scientists, writing in [Nature Biotechnology](/wiki/Nature_Biotechnology "Nature Biotechnology"), have disagreed.[[3]](#cite_note-Schubert06-3) In 2012 the [European Food Safety Authority](/wiki/European_Food_Safety_Authority "European Food Safety Authority") (EFSA) issued a report with their risk assessment of cisgenic and intragenic plants. They compared the hazards associated with plants produced by cisgenesis and intragenesis with those obtained either by conventional plant breeding techniques or transgenesis. The EFSA concluded that "similar hazards can be associated with cisgenic and conventionally bred plants, while novel hazards can be associated with intragenic and transgenic plants."[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Cisgenesis has been applied to transfer of natural resistance genes to the devastating disease *[Phytophthora infestans](/wiki/Phytophthora_infestans "Phytophthora infestans")* in potato[[7]](#cite_note-7) and [scab](/wiki/Apple_scab "Apple scab") (*[Venturia inaequalis](/wiki/Venturia_inaequalis "Venturia inaequalis")*) in apple.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Cisgenesis and transgenesis use artificial [gene transfer](/wiki/Horizontal_gene_transfer "Horizontal gene transfer"), which results in less extensive change to an organism's genome than [mutagenesis](/wiki/Mutagenesis "Mutagenesis"), which was widely used before genetic engineering was developed.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Some people believe that cisgenesis should not face as much regulatory oversight as [genetic modification](/wiki/Genetic_modification "Genetic modification") created through transgenesis as it is possible, if not practical, to transfer alleles among closely related species even by traditional crossing. The primary biological advantage of cisgenesis is that it does not disrupt favorable heterozygous states, particularly in asexually propagated crops such as potato, which do not breed true to seed. One application of cisgenesis is to create [blight](/wiki/Potato_blight "Potato blight") resistant potato plants by transferring known resistance loci wild genotypes into modern, high yielding varieties.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Dutch government has proposed to exclude cisgenic plants from the European GMO Regulation, in view of the safety of cisgenic plants compared to classically bred plants, and their contribution to durable food production.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Related classification scheme

A related classification scheme proposed by Kaare Nielsen is:[[1]](#cite_note-Nielsen03-1)

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Source of genetic modification | Genetic variability via conventional breeding | Genetic distance |
| Intragenic | Within genome | Possible | Low |
| Famigenic | Species in the same family | Possible |  |
| Linegenic | Species in the same lineage | Impossible |  |
| Transgenic | Unrelated species | Impossible |  |
| Xenogenic | Laboratory-designed genes | Impossible | High |

--- SOURCE 3: Cisgenesis: an important sub-invention for traditional plant ... ---
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10681-009-0037-y

SUMMARY:
por E Jacobsen · 2009 · Mencionado por 76 — Cisgenesis is the combined use of only cisgenes with marker-free transformation, mimicking linkage drag free introgression breeding in one step.

--- SOURCE 4: Cisgenic plants are similar to traditionally bred plants ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1525145/

SUMMARY:
por HJ Schouten · 2006 · Mencionado por 582 — Cisgenesis is a particularly efficient method for cross-fertilizing heterozygous plants that propagate vegetatively, such as potato, apple and banana.

As an alternative, cisgenesis and intragenesis are modern plant breeding approaches that can act upon intermediate to transgenic and traditional breeding method

--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: http://image.slidesharecdn.com/transgenesisintragenesiscisgenesis-150309072833-conversion-gate01/95/transgenesis-intragenesis-and-cisgenesis-a-brief-review-4-638.jpg?cb=1425886260
DESCRIPTION: The image explains the concept of conventional plant breeding, highlighting techniques like hybrid breeding, bridge crossing, and backcrossing, with diagrams illustrating the processes and challenges such as undesirable traits and wild characteristics.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://images.tpointtech.com/definition/images/plant-breeding-definition.png
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts the process of cisgenesis in plant breeding, starting with bacteria used for DNA extraction and isolation, followed by cloning and designing genes, then transformation into plant tissues via cell and tissue culture, culminating in the development of genetically modified plants through integrated steps.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/saurabh2ppt-140911012235-phpapp01/95/cisgenesis-and-intragenesis-by-saurabh-38-638.jpg?cb=1414839476
DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates the process of Agrobacterium tumefaciens-mediated gene transfer, showing steps such as in vitro plant preparation, leaf explant cutting, incubation with Agrobacterium, coculture and regeneration, selection of transgenic shoots, rooting, and acclimatization to greenhouse conditions.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://image5.slideserve.com/9435830/definition-l.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image displays a slide with the title "Definition" that explains cisgenesis in plant breeding, highlighting that it involves genetically modifying a plant with genes from a related, crossable plant, using naturally occurring and complete genes, without foreign genes, and that it could be developed through classical breeding.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/259608502/figure/fig1/AS:202780275941379@1425357993408/Comparison-between-Cisgene-and-transgene-technology-Source-Schouten-et-al-5.png
DESCRIPTION: None

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cisgenesis involves transferring genes between closely related plants, mimicking natural breeding methods, ensuring only desirable traits are introduced without foreign DNA.
This technique preserves the genetic integrity of the recipient plant by avoiding disruptive modifications, ensuring that beneficial alleles are integrated without altering existing favorable traits.

enriched_points.point_title: Public acceptance compared to traditional GMOs
enriched_points.expanded_content: Cisgenesis faces less public resistance than traditional GMOs, as it utilizes genes from closely related species, resembling conventional breeding, thus promoting broader acceptance.
